content.full_text:
FAIR p OLITICAL p RACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Kristina Raspe 
Vice President 
September 6, 2011 
Real Estate Development and Asser Management 
University of Southern California 
Email: kraspe@re.usc.edu 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-11-166 
Dear Ms. Raspe: 
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the duties of Rick Caruso, a 
member of the University of Southern California Board of Trustees and the University of 
Southern California's member on the Los Angeles Coliseum Commission ("LACC") under the 
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 You asked us for 
expedited advice. 
As you are aware, our advice is based solely on provisions of the Act. We therefore offer 
no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law 
conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. Also note, our advice is based solely on 
the facts presented in your request; the Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it 
provides advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Does Mr. Caruso have a conflict of interest under the Act in his decisions as a member of 
Los Angeles Coliseum Commission regarding a lease in which the University of Southern 
California is a party by virtue of his membership on the Board of Trustees of the University of 
Southern California? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The Regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
Our File No. A-11-166 
Page 2 
Under your facts, it does-not appear that Mr. Caruso has any economic interest in the 
University of Southern California ("USC") for purposes of the Act. Thus, Mr. Caruso is not 
prohibited by the Act from participating in decisions regarding USC as a member of the LACC. 
FACTS 
Mr. Caruso is a member of the USC Board of Trustees and is also USC's member on the 
LACC. Several years ago, USC negotiated a long-term lease of the Los Angeles Memorial 
Coliseum with the LACC. LACC is a joint powers agency comprised of the City of Los 
Angeles, the County of Los Angeles, and the State of California. One of the terms of the new 
lease requires that a USC nominated person be given a seat on the nine-member commission 
throughout the term of the lease. USC's current representative is Rick Caruso. Mr. Caruso was 
appointed to the LACC several years ago in accordance with the lease and an amendment to the 
JPA, which was approved by its members (the City of Los Angeles, the County of Los Angeles, 
and the State of California). 
The LACC is currently considering the current lease agreement and some members of the 
LACC want to propose that the LACC enter into a long-term master lease allowing USC to 
maintain and operate the facility. The matter is intended to be discussed and voted on in the 
Commission's closed session on September 7, 2011. In anticipation of this discussion and vote, 
Los Angeles City Councilmember Bernard Parks has questioned the ability of Rick Caruso to 
participate in the discussion and vote. You seek our advice as to whether Mr. Caruso has a 
conflict of interest under the Act due to his role as a trustee of USC. 
In a telephone conversation with your office on September 6, 2011, you confirmed that 
Mr. Caruso does not receive compensation or per diem as a trustee for USC and that USC is a 
501 ( c )(3) educational institution. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has g_ 
financial interest.2 As a member of the LACC, Mr. Caruso is a public official for purposes of 
the Act. (Section 82048; Regulation 18701.) 
The conflict of interest analysis under the Act consists of several steps, starting with the 
requirement that the public official must be making, participating in making, or using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision, in which it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect on the official, a member of the official's 
2 In this respect, the Act's conflict of interest provisions is narrower than the common law conflict of inter­
est rules and Section I 090. 


Our File No. A-11-166 
Page 3 
immediate family, or on any one of the following six statutorily identified economic interests of 
the official: 
• An interest in a business entity in which a public official has a direct or indirect 
investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a), Regulation 18703.l(a).) 
• An interest in a business entity in which a public official is a director, officer, partner, 
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d), 
Regulation 18703.l(b).) 
• An interest in real property in which a public official has a direct or indirect interest 
of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b), Regulation 18703.2.) 
• Any source of income to the public official which aggregates to $500 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c), Regulation 18703.3.) 
• Any source of gifts to the public official if the gifts aggregate to $420 or more within 
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e), Regulation 18703.4.) 
• An in his or her personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of 
his or her immediate family. (Section 87103, Regulation 18703.5.) 
Under your facts, it does not appear that Mr. Caruso has any economic interest in USC 
that fits within the statutory economic interests specified above. USC is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit 
organization, not a bqsiness entity.3 (Section 82005.) Thus, he would not have an interest in 
USC by virtue of Section 87103(d). Moreover, as a volunteer trustee, he receives no income 
from USC. Therefore, USC is not a source of income to Mr. Caruso. Finally, you have 
presented no facts indicating that participating in these decisions would have a personal financial 
effect on him. Therefore, Mr. Caruso is not prohibited by the Act from participating in decisions 
regarding USC as a member of the LACC. 
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660. 
Sincerely, 
Zackery Morazzini 
ral Counsel 
JWW:jgl 
3 Section 82005 defines "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for profit, including but 
not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association

prohibited by the Act from participating in decisions regarding USC as a member of the LACC.
